concept.title: Authoring DITA Topics
concept.shortdesc: Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It's writing with:

section.title: What “authoring in DITA” really means
section.p: Writing DITA is not just writing in XML. It’s writing with:

ul.li: a content model (what elements are allowed where),
a purpose (concept vs task vs reference),
a reuse strategy (what stays local vs shared),
a governance strategy (consistent attributes, IDs, terminology).

section.p: When DITA authoring goes wrong, it’s usually not because XML is hard. It’s because the team never agreed on:
This topic gives you a practical, “how real teams do it” guide.

section.title: Topic design: picking the right topic type
section.p: Use concept topics to explain:

ul.li: “when to use” and “when not to use.”

section.p: A good concept topic:
Common elements:
Use tasks to instruct:
A good task topic:

ul.li: ends with results or next steps.

section.p: Common elements:
Use reference topics for:
A good reference topic:

section.title: The most important authoring decision: topic scope (“chunking”)
section.p: Chunking is how you decide what is:

ul.li: a set of topics assembled by a map.

section.p: Chunking affects:

ul.li: the content solves one user goal,
the content can be reused elsewhere,
the content could appear in search results on its own.

p.b: Make a section (not a new topic) when:

ul.li: the content is only meaningful in the same context,
splitting would create “orphan topics” with unclear purpose.
it becomes too long to scan,
it mixes concept and procedure heavily,
it gets reused partially (a sign you need smaller building blocks).

section.p: If a topic title needs “and” or “then” often, consider splitting:

ul.li: “Install and Configure the System” → likely two tasks or a concept + task.
“What It Is and How to Use It” → concept + task.

section.title: IDs, filenames, and stability (treat your content like an API)

p.b: Why IDs matter more than you think

section.p: DITA references rely on IDs:
If IDs change frequently, you break:

ul.li: Avoid “v2” or dates unless truly necessary
Match the filename or a stable semantic label:

section.p: - file: install.dita - topic id: install

ul.li: Avoid auto-generated IDs like topic_1234 if you can
Use meaningful IDs for reusable blocks:

section.p: - install-note - admin-warning - prereq-java

ul.li: Treat changing an ID like changing a public API:

section.p: - do it rarely, - communicate it, - update dependent references, - validate builds.

section.title: Writing style in DITA (structure first, prose second)
section.p: DITA is not a prose-first system. It’s structure-first:

ul.li: the structure communicates intent to both humans and machines,
the prose fills in the details.
Put expected results in <result> or a final step.
Separate UI and command line procedures if they diverge significantly.
Start with a crisp definition or summary.
Avoid hidden tasks (“Do X, do Y”) unless it’s truly conceptual.
Use stable ordering (alphabetical or logical grouping).

section.title: Practical authoring templates (copy/paste patterns)
section.codeblock:
... One-sentence summary for search and previews.

Definition and context.

Key idea #1.

Key idea #2.

Example

Use an example to clarify.

... What this procedure achieves.

Conditions and required permissions.

When and why you do this.

 Do step 1. Do step 2. Verify the result.

What success looks like.

<reference id="..."> <title>...</title> <shortdesc>What this reference covers.</shortdesc> <refbody> <section> <title>Syntax</title> <codeblock>command --option value</codeblock> </section> <section> <title>Options</title> <simpletable> <sthead> <stentry>Option</stentry> <stentry>Description</stentry> </sthead> <strow> <stentry><codeph>--help</codeph></stentry> <stentry>Displays help text.</stentry> </strow> </simpletable> </section> </refbody> </reference>

section.title: Authoring for reuse: designing “reusable chunks”
section.p: Reuse is not free. It requires deliberate boundaries.

ul.li: standard procedures used across many tasks,

p.b: What should usually NOT be reused?

ul.li: content that’s meaningful only in one location,
deep cross-referenced paragraphs that assume surrounding text.

section.p: A reusable chunk should:
If it depends on context, keep it local.

section.title: Metadata: the overlooked authoring power tool
section.p: Metadata improves:
Create a “metadata minimum standard”:

ul.li: every topic must have <shortdesc> and at least one keyword,
every task must include prerequisites if relevant,
every reference must include syntax and examples.

section.p: These standards reduce variability.

section.title: Images, UI screenshots, and media management

ul.li: Keep images in a dedicated folder: media/images/.
Use alt text and/or text equivalents where required.
Prefer vector (SVG) where your pipeline supports it and where clarity matters.

section.p: Screenshots are expensive to maintain. DITA doesn’t solve that automatically. To reduce screenshot maintenance:

ul.li: document UI actions in text with <uicontrol> elements,

section.title: Translation and localization implications
section.p: DITA’s structure can reduce translation cost, but only if you:

ul.li: reuse content instead of duplicating it,
Don’t embed locale-specific text in shared snippets unless that’s intentional.
Use keys for product names and terms that differ by region.
Avoid hard-coded date formats and culturally specific examples in globally reused topics.

section.title: Common authoring mistakes (and how to fix them)
section.p: Fix: adopt information typing (concept/task/reference) and enforce it via review.
Fix: split into smaller topics and assemble with maps.
Fix: reuse only stable, context-independent chunks; document ownership and change control.
Fix: define a controlled vocabulary and enforce it (subject scheme + process).
Fix: treat IDs as stable contracts; manage renames with care.

section.title: A practical authoring workflow for teams
section.p: Here’s a workflow that works for both file-based and CCMS environments:

ol.li: Plan: create map skeleton (TOC) and topic list.
Draft: create topic stubs with titles and shortdesc.
Review: SME + editorial; check structure and semantics.
Reuse pass: consolidate repeated content into reuse library.
Validate: run builds and resolve warnings/errors.
Publish: produce HTML/PDF; confirm navigation and search behavior.
Maintain: version and update with stable IDs and reuse targets.

section.title: Action checklist (do this to get productive quickly)

ul.li: [ ] Choose topic types you will use (start with concept/task/reference).
[ ] Define naming conventions for files and IDs.
[ ] Create templates and enforce them (even if informally at first).
[ ] Establish reuse boundaries and a reuse folder.
[ ] Run validation on every change (locally or in CI).
[ ] Keep topics small and stable; assemble with maps.

section.title: Analytics and Instrumentation
section.p: Track authoring patterns and template usage with analytics. Monitor which templates are most used and where authors encounter issues. Use this data to improve authoring workflows and training.

section.title: Personalization and Conditional Processing
section.p: Use DITA conditional attributes to tailor authoring guidance for different roles or workflows. Example:

section.title: Integrating with Conversational AI and NLP
section.p: Expose authoring best practices to chatbots or virtual assistants. Enable users to ask for authoring help and receive context-aware answers from this topic.

section.title: Continuous Improvement and Feedback Loops
section.p: Collect feedback from authors and editors on templates and workflows. Use analytics and feedback to refine authoring guidance and improve documentation quality.
